scope.setting: finite two-observer static-patch screen benchmark with shared horizon algebra C^d
scope.result_type: screen-shadow no-go plus intrinsic completion principle
scope.claim_boundary: finite QEC/OA-QEC compatibility benchmark; not continuum dS/CFT, de Sitter quantum gravity, type-III algebra, or ER=EPR in de Sitter

core_claim: Finite dS/CFT-visible screen shadows restricted to a shared horizon algebra can agree while algebraic ER=EPR bridge connectivity differs.

exact_theorem_style_claims.claim: For every d>=2, identity on M_d and complete dephasing onto C^d have identical screen entropies, diagonal correlator shadows, horizon-overlap data, and transfer spectra restricted to C^d, while their maximal recoverable bridge algebras differ.
Off-diagonal response, an informationally complete operator atlas, or an equivalent maximality test separates the bridge channels in this family.
At screen probability p=0.75, north and south screen entropies tie while channel recovery favors north.

relationship_to_prior_goals.goal19: Algebraic connectivity is recoverable bridge algebra detected by response plus closure when the probe atlas is complete.
relationship_to_prior_goals.goal20: Probe incompleteness lets diagonal response certify C^d without detecting whether M_d is recoverable.
relationship_to_prior_goals.goal21: The same obstruction becomes a finite dS/CFT compatibility benchmark: horizon-screen shadows can hide the ER=EPR bridge algebra.

proof_obligations.claim: Emit the Goal 21 finite dS/CFT-ER=EPR compatibility certificate.
Validate the machine-readable Goal 21 certificate index.

expert_feedback_summary: In a finite two-observer static-patch benchmark, dS/CFT-like screen data restricted to the shared horizon do not determine algebraic ER=EPR connectivity. The missing datum is intrinsic observer-algebra response/commutator tomography.
limitations: This is finite QEC/OA-QEC mathematics. It does not construct a continuum CFT dual, de Sitter quantum gravity, type-III observer algebra, or literal ER=EPR-in-dS theorem.
